title: Return Values 🎁
description: Understand Ruby's magical implicit return system.

slides.title: Feeding Data Back ⬅️
slides.content:
So far, our methods have been a one-way street. We feed data in, and it usually just `puts` something to the screen.

But methods can actually **Return** data back to the rest of your program!

This is the most powerful feature of methods.

slides.title: The `return` Keyword
slides.content:
You can instruct a method to spit data back out using the `return` keyword.
When you call the method, it transforms into the data it returned!

```ruby
def add_five(number)
  return number + 5
end

result = add_five(5)
puts result # Prints 10
```

slides.title: Storing Returns
slides.content: Notice how we aren't using `puts` inside the method. The method simply hands data back, which we then save inside the variable `double`.
slides.codeSnippet:
def multiplier(a, b)
  return a * b
end

double = multiplier(10, 2)
puts double

quizData.question: Which keyword officially spits data back out of a method to be used by the rest of the program?
quizData.explanation: The `return` keyword formally terminates the method and hands the value back out.

slides.title: Implicit Returns 🪄
slides.content:
# Ruby Magic!

Ruby has a gorgeous feature called **Implicit Return**.

A method in Ruby will ALWAYS return the last evaluated expression, even if you don't type the word `return`! In fact, typing the word `return` is highly discouraged inside the Ruby community.

slides.title: Invisible Returns
slides.content: Run this! Because `a + b` is the absolute last line of code inside the method, Ruby automatically returns that exact value.
slides.codeSnippet:
def implicit_add(a, b)
  # Look ma, no 'return' keyword!
  a + b
end

ans = implicit_add(10, 5)
puts ans

quizData.question: If you don't write the `return` keyword, what does a Ruby method return by default?

options.text: The result of the very last line of code executed.

quizData.explanation: Ruby naturally evaluates the very last line of a method and spits that final piece of data out, magically. It results in extremely elegant code.

slides.title: Return Destroys the Method
slides.content: Just like the `break` keyword in loops, the second Ruby hits a `return` keyword (implicit or explicit), the method terminates permanently. Any code below a `return` is dead code entirely.
